text: I consistently volunteer my expertise to help others solve their problems, even when I receive nothing in return.
I feel that the most valuable thing I own is my willingness to share it.
I would rather mentor someone else's talent than develop a new skill of my own.
I believe my knowledge is wasted if it only benefits me.
I instinctively offer my seat, my place in line, or my turn to someone else.
I feel that keeping a windfall for myself when others are in need would be wrong.
I tend to craft my daily schedule around what the people in my life need from me.
I find that cooking for others, even when I am tired, fills me with purpose.
I believe my professional network exists primarily so I can connect people who need each other.
I would choose a lower-paying role that lets me serve my community over a higher-paying one that does not.
I routinely stay late to help a colleague finish their work, even when mine is already done.
I feel that my home should always be open to anyone who needs a place to stay.
I find that writing a recommendation letter for someone else brings me more satisfaction than updating my own resume.
I hold the view that my car, my tools, and my space are shared assets for anyone who needs them.
I would skip my own vacation to cover for a coworker dealing with a family crisis.
I believe every conversation should end with the other person feeling more supported than before.
I tend to carry the heaviest bag, take the longest walk, or accept the least comfortable seat without being asked.
I feel that lending money I may never get back is preferable to watching someone struggle.
I find that anonymously paying for a stranger's meal is one of my favorite things to do.
I hold the view that my education was given to me so I could teach others.
I routinely offer to drive others to appointments, even when it disrupts my own plans.
I believe that if I have a roof over my head and someone else does not, something is mine to give.
I feel most purposeful when I am tutoring, coaching, or guiding someone through a challenge.
I tend to give the largest portion to others and take whatever remains.
I find that sharing credit publicly while absorbing blame privately is my natural style.
I would downsize my own living situation to free up resources for people who need help.
I believe that when someone asks me for help, the answer should almost always be yes.
I hold the view that unused capacity in my life represents a missed opportunity to serve.
I feel that passing along an opportunity to someone better suited, even when I want it, is the right call.
I routinely spend my lunch break helping others with their tasks instead of resting.
I tend to buy gifts for others before buying things I need for myself.
I find that the stories I most love telling are about times I was able to help someone.
I believe my physical energy should be spent on tasks that make life easier for those around me.
I would take on a second job to support a family member's dream.
I feel that any privilege I hold comes with a duty to redistribute it.
I hold the view that my weekends are better spent volunteering than relaxing.
I routinely check in on neighbors, acquaintances, and distant friends to make sure they are okay.
I find that when I receive a compliment, my first thought is who else deserves one more.
I believe my calendar should reflect other people's needs as much as my own.
I tend to put others' comfort ahead of efficiency when the two conflict.
I feel that the most important line item in my budget is what I give away.
I would forgo a hobby I love if the time could be better spent helping my community.
I hold the view that having more than I need while others have less is inherently imbalanced.
I routinely proofread, edit, or review other people's work without being asked.
I find that organizing events for others brings me more joy than attending events for myself.
I believe my social media presence should amplify others' voices, not just my own.
I tend to donate my old belongings rather than sell them, even when I could use the money.
I feel that celebrating someone else's achievement is more satisfying than celebrating my own.
I would walk so someone else could ride.
I hold the view that every skill I develop should eventually be taught to someone else.
I routinely absorb extra costs in group situations so others pay less.
I find that my instinct when I hear about someone's problem is to offer a concrete solution, not just sympathy.
I believe that being the person who cleans up after everyone else is a form of quiet leadership.
I tend to take on the overnight shift or the early morning slot so others can have the easier schedule.
I feel that my savings account is also an emergency fund for the people I care about.
I would trade a comfortable retirement for the chance to fund someone else's education.
I hold the view that the best use of any bonus or raise is to share it.
I routinely carry extra supplies, snacks, or resources in case someone around me needs them.
I find that when I shop for myself, I end up buying things for other people instead.
I believe that the effort I put into making others successful is more valuable than the effort I put into my own success.
I tend to let others choose first—the restaurant, the movie, the vacation destination.
I feel that my role in any friendship is to be the one who gives more.
I would relocate to be closer to someone who needs my daily support.
I hold the view that I should be the first to arrive and the last to leave when there is work to be done for others.
I routinely translate, explain, or simplify information so others can access it.
I find that fixing something in someone else's home or workspace is deeply gratifying.
I believe that when I succeed, the first thing I should do is figure out how to bring others along.
I tend to undercharge or waive fees when someone genuinely cannot afford what I offer.
I feel that keeping my accomplishments quiet so others can feel proud of theirs is a worthy trade.
I would invest my inheritance in a cause rather than spend it on myself.
I hold the view that my free time is a resource that should flow toward whoever needs it most.
I routinely adapt my preferences to accommodate the needs of those around me.
I find that assembling care packages for people going through hard times comes naturally to me.
I believe that the connections I build should create value for others, not just for me.
I tend to pick up the tab without mentioning it.
I feel that my daily to-do list should have more items for others than for myself.
I would sacrifice a personal milestone to attend to someone else's urgent need.
I hold the view that if I can solve a problem for someone else, I am obligated to try.
I routinely go out of my way to introduce people to opportunities they deserve.
I find that spending an afternoon helping a friend move is more rewarding than spending it on my own pursuits.
I believe my workspace should be arranged so that others can use it comfortably, not just me.
I tend to set my thermostat, my music, and my lighting to what makes my household most comfortable, not just me.
I feel that every talent I have is a tool meant to benefit other people.
I would turn down a dream job if accepting it meant abandoning someone who depends on me.
I hold the view that generosity with my time is the highest form of generosity.
I routinely take on administrative and logistical work so that others can focus on what they do best.
I find that remembering small details about other people's lives and acting on them is one of my strengths.
I believe it is better to overcommit to helping others than to undercommit.
I tend to share my food, my space, and my belongings without a second thought.
I feel that I should always leave a situation better for the next person.
I would cancel a personal trip to help a neighbor through an emergency.
I hold the view that the best friendships are the ones where I give more than I get.
I routinely write thoughtful, detailed feedback for others' projects even when no one asks.
I find that when I have a choice between advancing myself and supporting someone else, I choose the latter.
I believe that hosting others in my home, even at personal inconvenience, is an essential practice.
I tend to plan surprises and gestures for the people around me more than for myself.
I feel that my proudest moments are when someone tells me I made a difference in their life.
I would spend my sabbatical working on behalf of a community project rather than resting.
I hold the view that the surplus in my life exists so that I can fill the deficit in someone else's.
I routinely adjust my pace, my route, or my method to match what works best for others.
I find that advocating for someone else's raise or promotion feels as important as pursuing my own.
I believe that the right response to abundance is to distribute it, not to hoard it.
I tend to prioritize errands for others over my own errands when time is limited.
I feel that my legacy should be defined by the people I helped, not the things I acquired.
I would use my professional influence to open doors for others rather than to advance my own agenda.
I hold the view that the proper response to receiving help is to immediately look for someone else to help.
I routinely reduce my own workload expectations so I can pitch in where others are struggling.
I find that deferring my desires so a group can reach consensus comes easily to me.
I believe that if my plate is full and someone else's is empty, something on my plate belongs to them.
I tend to choose the harder path if it clears the way for someone else.
I feel that each day I do not contribute to someone else's well-being is a day partially wasted.
I would trade personal recognition for the satisfaction of knowing my efforts lifted the team.
I hold the view that the simplest acts of service—holding a door, carrying a bag—are among the most meaningful.
I routinely offer to handle the least glamorous tasks in any group effort.
I find that my impulse when I learn something new is to teach it to someone who could benefit.
I believe that personal convenience should never come at the expense of someone else's basic need.
I tend to respond to every request for help before attending to my own inbox.
I feel that contributing to a shared meal is more fulfilling than dining alone on something better.
I would give up a front-row seat so a stranger could have a better experience.
I hold the view that the measure of my character is how I treat people who can offer me nothing.
I routinely set aside a portion of every paycheck specifically for helping others.
I find that building something for someone else's use is more motivating than building it for my own.
I believe that the discomfort of giving is always worth the benefit it creates for someone else.
I tend to consider how my choices affect the most vulnerable people around me before deciding.
I feel that an open hand is always preferable to a closed fist, even when closing would protect my own interests.
I would share a professional secret or competitive advantage if it would significantly help someone else.
I hold the view that comfort shared is comfort doubled, and comfort hoarded is comfort halved.
I routinely pick up litter, fix broken things, and tidy shared spaces without being asked.
I find that spending my birthday doing something for others feels more special than celebrating myself.
I believe that my strength exists to be lent to those who need it.
I tend to put my phone away and give my full presence to whoever is in front of me.
I feel that if I have the ability to ease someone's burden, hesitation is unacceptable.
I would deplete my emergency fund to help a loved one avoid a crisis.
I hold the view that the world needs more people who give without calculating what they get back.
I routinely hand off leads, clients, or referrals to colleagues who need them more.
I find that simplifying my own life so I can be more available to others is deeply rewarding.
I believe that every act of generosity, no matter how small, matters more than any personal gain.
I tend to fill gaps in any group without being asked—whatever is missing, I provide.
I feel that my energy is best spent making sure nobody around me is struggling alone.
I would choose a less prestigious school or job if the alternative let me stay close to people who need me.
I hold the view that unused privilege is wasted privilege.
I routinely bring extra food, supplies, or materials to share with whoever might need them.
I find that being the person others turn to in a crisis is one of the most meaningful roles I play.
I believe that investing in someone else's potential is more important than polishing my own resume.
I find that tracking my personal milestones keeps me motivated and on course.
I believe that curating my own experiences is one of the richest parts of life.
I hold the view that my morning routine should be designed entirely around my own optimal performance.
I feel that reserving my best energy for my own creative work is non-negotiable.
I tend to choose the restaurant, the movie, or the activity that I most want.
I routinely update my personal goals and adjust my life to match them.
I believe that a well-furnished personal space is a worthwhile investment.
I find that maintaining a personal journal focused on my own progress is deeply satisfying.
I hold the view that my hobbies deserve as much time as any obligation to others.
I feel that buying the best tools and equipment for myself directly improves my output.
I tend to accept only the projects that align with my personal vision.
I routinely turn down invitations that do not serve my personal interests or growth.
I believe that building a personal portfolio of accomplishments is essential.
I find that my most productive days are those entirely devoted to my own agenda.
I hold the view that personal rituals and routines are sacred and should not be disrupted by others' requests.
I feel that splurging on a personal passion project is money well spent.
I tend to measure my week by how much I advanced my own objectives.
I routinely reserve the best seat, the best portion, or the best option for myself.
I believe that I function best when I have dedicated, uninterrupted time for my own work.
I find that charting my personal growth on paper gives me a tangible sense of progress.
I hold the view that my personal library, workspace, and equipment should reflect my standards.
I feel that dressing well and presenting myself at my best is an investment in my future.
I tend to pick vacations based on what I want to see and do, not on what others prefer.
I routinely block off calendar time that belongs exclusively to me.
I believe that hiring help for tasks I dislike frees me to focus on what I do best.
I find that saying no to requests that dilute my focus is one of my most valuable habits.
I hold the view that my own intellectual curiosity should guide how I spend my learning time.
I feel that cultivating personal expertise in my chosen field is a lifelong priority.
I tend to negotiate the arrangement that works best for me in any shared situation.
I routinely upgrade my personal environment—better lighting, better furniture, better technology.
I believe that tracking my own financial growth is a satisfying and important practice.
I find that designing my ideal morning, without accommodation for anyone else, sets me up for success.
I hold the view that my dietary choices should reflect my own health goals, not others' preferences.
I feel that personal reading time is a non-negotiable part of my schedule.
I tend to keep my most innovative ideas for my own ventures.
I routinely evaluate friendships by whether they contribute to my personal development.
I believe that my physical fitness routine should take priority over social commitments.
I find that eating alone at my favorite restaurant is a perfectly worthwhile way to spend an evening.
I hold the view that my long-term wealth is one of the most important things I am building.
I feel that taking a solo trip to recharge is more valuable than a group vacation.
I tend to choose the apartment, the neighborhood, and the city that best serve my own goals.
I routinely set personal records and benchmarks to push myself further.
I believe that my taste, my preferences, and my style deserve full expression.
I find that competing against my own previous best is one of the most motivating things I do.
I hold the view that protecting my sleep schedule is more important than being available to others late at night.
I feel that building a personal reputation for excellence is one of my top priorities.
I tend to use unexpected free time for personal projects rather than reaching out to see if someone needs help.
I routinely rearrange my environment to maximize my own comfort and productivity.
I believe that accumulating experiences that enrich my life is a valid use of resources.
I find that setting aggressive personal deadlines keeps me sharp and engaged.
I hold the view that I should not apologize for wanting the best for myself.
I feel that my creative projects deserve the same level of investment I would give to a professional obligation.
I tend to structure group decisions around outcomes that benefit me most.
I routinely invest in courses, certifications, and credentials that advance my career.
I believe that my personal brand—how I am perceived—is something I should actively manage.
I find that choosing my own comfort over shared inconvenience is usually the right call.
I hold the view that my retirement planning should come before discretionary generosity.
I feel that working on my own side projects in the evenings is more rewarding than socializing.
I tend to ask for the raise, the upgrade, or the better deal without hesitation.
I routinely choose the path of least resistance for myself, even if it requires more from others.
I believe that being well-rested, well-fed, and well-prepared is my responsibility to myself.
I find that I naturally prioritize tasks that build my own skill set over tasks that only help others.
I hold the view that collecting meaningful personal possessions adds richness to my life.
I feel that earning more money for myself is one of the most practical goals I can pursue.
I tend to see networking as a way to build my own opportunities, first and foremost.
I routinely audit my commitments and drop those that no longer serve my personal direction.
I believe that the quality of my personal environment directly affects the quality of my thinking.
I find that setting firm boundaries around my personal time prevents burnout and keeps me effective.
I hold the view that personal ambition and drive are qualities to be proud of, not downplayed.
I feel that optimizing my workflow for my own efficiency is a top priority.
I tend to choose my battles based on what matters most to my own goals.
I routinely treat myself to experiences and products that bring me personal joy.
I believe that my personal development plan should be revisited and refined regularly.
I find that decorating my space exactly to my taste improves my daily mood and output.
I hold the view that time spent perfecting my craft is never wasted.
I feel that guarding my creative energy from others' demands is essential to producing my best work.
I tend to make major life decisions based primarily on what excites and fulfills me.
I routinely research and pursue opportunities that specifically strengthen my resume or portfolio.
I believe that the time I spend alone working on my goals is among my most valuable hours.
I find that rewarding myself after completing a personal goal reinforces my discipline.
I hold the view that my personal reading list, podcast queue, and study plan should reflect only my interests.
I feel that my personal workspace should be optimized for my needs, not shared convenience.
I tend to choose the fastest lane, the shortest line, and the most efficient option for myself.
I routinely calculate the return on investment for my personal time and money.
I believe that developing a signature style—in work, dress, and communication—is important for my identity.
I find that competing with others for top positions energizes me.
I hold the view that my own legacy—what I build and leave behind—is a primary concern.
I feel that hiring a personal trainer, coach, or therapist is an investment in my most important asset: myself.
I tend to evaluate any group activity by asking what I personally stand to gain.
I routinely keep a list of personal achievements and review it for motivation.
I believe that my personal standards for quality should never be compromised to accommodate others.
I find that traveling alone to pursue my own interests is more fulfilling than compromising on a group itinerary.
I hold the view that my financial independence is one of the most important things I can achieve.
I feel that my exercise time, my meal preparation, and my rest are boundaries I protect fiercely.
I tend to frame career conversations around my own aspirations and what I need to get there.
I routinely stockpile resources—savings, knowledge, connections—for my own future use.
I believe that my personal comfort zone should expand on my terms, not because someone else pushed me.
I find that building something I am personally proud of is the strongest motivator I know.
I hold the view that self-improvement books, courses, and retreats are among my best investments.
I feel that securing the best possible outcome for myself in a negotiation is simply smart.
I tend to exit commitments that no longer align with where I want to go personally.
I routinely visualize my own future success and make decisions to bring that vision closer.
I believe that my unique talents are best expressed through projects I choose for myself.
I find that maximizing my own earning potential is a responsibility I take seriously.
I hold the view that personal benchmarks—income, fitness, skills—are the truest measures of progress.
I feel that my daily habits should be engineered to serve my long-term personal vision.
I tend to choose mentors and advisors based on how they can accelerate my personal growth.
I routinely protect my focus time even when others express disappointment.
I believe that taking the most interesting assignment for myself, rather than the most needed one, is fair.
I find that my personal satisfaction with my work matters more to me than external validation.
I hold the view that I deserve to enjoy the lifestyle my work has earned.
I feel that refining my personal aesthetic—in everything from clothing to home design—is a worthy pursuit.
I tend to use windfalls on personal upgrades rather than distributing them to others.
I routinely document my own accomplishments so they are recognized and remembered.
I believe that my personal energy is a finite resource that I should allocate with deliberate intention toward my own goals.
I find that building a strong personal financial cushion gives me more confidence than anything else.

I feel that my own creative output is the most important thing I produce.
I tend to choose conferences, workshops, and events based on how they advance my career specifically.
I routinely assess whether my current role still serves my personal growth trajectory.
I believe that personal achievement is the foundation upon which everything else in my life rests.
I find that having a detailed personal five-year plan gives me clarity and drive.
I hold the view that my own needs are the baseline I should secure before extending help outward.
I feel that pursuing mastery in my chosen field is worth more than diversifying my efforts for others.
I tend to keep my best contacts, resources, and strategies within my own network for my own use.
I routinely upgrade my skills specifically to increase my market value.
I believe that the hours between 6 a.m. and 9 a.m. should belong entirely to my personal development.
I find that maintaining a wish list of things I want for myself keeps me motivated.
I hold the view that being strategically selfish is a prerequisite for long-term effectiveness.
I feel that my personal taste in food, travel, and entertainment should guide shared decisions.
I tend to frame every major decision by asking: does this move me closer to where I want to be?
I routinely prioritize my own deadlines over requests that come from others.
I believe that assembling a life that reflects my personal values and desires is the primary task of adulthood.
I find that the projects I initiate for myself are the ones I pour the most energy into.
I hold the view that self-advocacy is a skill everyone should develop, and I practice it constantly.
I feel that reserving weekends for personal restoration is essential, even when others want my time.

I believe that my most important competition is with my past self, and I intend to keep winning.
I find that aligning every aspect of my life with my personal mission statement keeps me centered.

I routinely choose solitude for deep personal work over collaboration that dilutes my focus.
I believe that cultivating my own inner life—through reflection, learning, and growth—is my highest duty.

I hold the view that any award or recognition I receive belongs partly to everyone who supported me.
